evals.name: closure-cn
evals.prompt: 解释一下 Python 的闭包，我每次看完就忘
evals.expected_output: Five-layer lesson in Chinese with a runnable Python closure example (make_counter style), a walkthrough, the late-binding-in-a-loop trap as L4, and 2-3 pointers in L5.

evals.name: cap-theorem-en
evals.prompt: explain the CAP theorem to me — i keep hearing about it but the wikipedia definition is useless
evals.expected_output: Five-layer lesson in English. Likely uses pseudocode rather than a real language (this is a protocol concept). L1 should frame the partition trade-off concretely. L4 should show what 'choosing C' vs 'choosing A' looks like during a partition.

evals.name: gil-cn
evals.prompt: Python 的 GIL 到底是什么？我以为多线程能加速但是好像没用
evals.expected_output: Five-layer lesson in Chinese, code in Python. L2 must contrast CPU-bound (threads don't help) vs I/O-bound (threads do help) with timing. L4 shows the wrong intuition the user already stated. L5 points at multiprocessing, asyncio, free-threaded Python.

evals.name: monad-en
evals.prompt: i've tried to understand monads four times. teach me with code that actually makes sense
evals.expected_output: Five-layer lesson in English. Code should NOT be Python (the concept loses too much). TypeScript or Haskell. L1 should frame monads as a *pattern for chaining computations that carry context* — not 'a monoid in the category of endofunctors'. L4 shows the trap of nested .then() / nested if-none chains that monads exist to flatten.

evals.name: grpo-cn
evals.prompt: 讲一下 GRPO，我看了几遍论文还是没搞懂它和 PPO 的区别
evals.expected_output: Five-layer lesson in Chinese. L2 MUST be PyTorch pseudocode (not runnable), with '# pseudocode' marker, [B, G] shape annotations for group-relative rewards, and a 2-3 line comparison with PPO (showing PPO uses learned value V vs GRPO uses group mean — that's the seam). L4 should hit a wrong intuition (e.g. 'GRPO is just PPO without a critic' is incomplete — it changes the variance/baseline story). L5 points to KL penalty, sampling group size, and DPO as an even-more-stripped alternative.

evals.name: moe-en
evals.prompt: i keep seeing 'Mixture of Experts' in model papers. explain how MoE actually works — what does 'routing' mean here?
evals.expected_output: Five-layer lesson in English. L2 MUST be PyTorch pseudocode (not runnable). MUST include shape transitions: [B, T, D] → [B, T, E] (gating) → [B, T, K] (top-k) → [N, D] (dispatched per expert) → [B, T, D] (combined). Must NOT collapse routing into a per-token for-loop (that's the wrong mental model). L4 shows the load-balancing trap (one expert hogging all tokens) or the wrong-intuition trap ('MoE = ensemble of experts averaged' — no, it's sparse routing). L5 points at auxiliary load-balancing loss, expert capacity, switch transformers.
